Explore physiological depressive symptoms profiles among Mexican-origin adolescents and its familial antecedents

Tianlu Zhang

2024-12-12

1 Introduction

1.1 Research question

Emerging research studying depression found that there are different types of depression in terms of the biographic symptoms.

Lamers et al.(2020) posits that there are four profiles of depression, within which “Immuno-metabolic depression” represents a particular ‘sub-type’ (see picture below) of depression characterized by distinct symptom profile (e.g., increased appetite and weight gain) and increased inflammatory and metabolically markers. Persons with immuno-metabolic depressive symptoms may be the most important group to target in prevention programs for cardiometabolic disease, and may benefit most from treatments targeting immuno-metabolic pathways. This is particularly important to study Latino adolescents as this group was found to suffered from metabolic & cardiovascular disease such as obesity.Therefore, it is important to recognize this group and explore the risk and protective factors for it.

1.3 Research aim

This study aims to identify the high-risk “Immuno-metabolic depression” in Mexican-origin adolescents with high biological & psychological risks by integrating biomakers (CRP,IL-6, HDL, A1C, systolic blood pressure, diastolic blood pressure, BMI percentiles) and self-report depressive symptom & anxiety data using latent profile analyses.

1.4 Hypotesis

Because the Latent Profile Analysis (LPA) is an exploratory technic. There is no specific hypothesis about the profile results. Rather, the current study expects there is a Immuno-metabolic depressive symptoms with high depressive symptoms, anxiety, high inflammation and metabolic risks. Besides that, there will also be a profile with high depressive symptoms and anxiety but no inflammation and metabolic risks, and another profile with high inflammation and metabolic risks but no high depressive symptoms and anxiety.

2 Methods

2.1 Data

The data for this study is from a three-wave longitudinal data across early (Wave 1: 2012 – 2015), middle (Wave 2: 2013 – 2016), to late adolescence (Wave 3: 2017 - 2020) focused on Mexican immigrant families in central Texas. Adolescents who provided dried blood spots (DBS) samples, blood pressure, and weight/height data during Wave 3 home visits were included in the current study. All variables using in latent profile analyses are from Wave 3

2.2 Variables

  • CRP_3SDWlg: 3-sd winsorized & log-transformed CRP (c-reaction protein)

  • IL_6plus1_lg: 3-sd winsorized & log-transformed IL-6 (XXX)

  • HDL_3SDWlg: 3-sd winsorized & log-transformed HDL (XXX)

  • BE_A1c_3SDWlg: 3-sd winsorized & log-transformed A1C (XXX)

  • t3bpsy_3SDWlg: 3-sd winsorized & log-transformed systolic blood pressure

  • t3bpdi_3SDWlg: 3-sd winsorized and log-transformed diastolic blood pressure

  • t3bmip_reco : BMI (Body Mass index) percentile/100

  • t3cesd: adolescent Wave 3 depresisve symptoms

  • t3anx: adolescent Wave 3 anxiety

    Inflammation biomakers include: CRP, IL-6 were (from DBS sample),Metabolic system biomakers include: HDL, A1C, blood pressure, BMI percentiles

2.3 Analyses methods

Latent Profile Analysis (LPA) is a statistical technique used to identify distinct subgroups (profiles) within a population based on continuous observed variables. LPA models the probability that an individual belongs to a latent class (profile) based on their observed values across several continuous variables. Each profile is characterized by its unique mean vector and variance-covariance structure for these variables.

3 Results

3.1 Data cleaning

3.1.1 Loading packages

library(tidyverse)
library(stringr)
library(dplyr)
library(MplusAutomation)
library(bruceR)
library(skimr)
library(kableExtra) #for table formatting
library(papaja) #for apa
## Warning: package 'papaja' was built under R version 4.4.0
library(table1)
library(tidyLPA) #for latent profile analyses
## Warning: package 'tidyLPA' was built under R version 4.4.0
library(scales)
library(DataExplorer)
library(foreign)
library(nnet) #nnet package to estimate a multinomial logistic regression model
library(ggplot2)
library(reshape2)

3.1.2 Importing & selecting samples for LPA

Data cleaning was conducted to include those individuals provided all biomakers - Because LPA could be biased when one participants not provide all varible values. This study only chooses those samples provided complete data in all variables

df <- import("./rprojectdata.sav")
#skim(df)
View(df)

#check the missing data patterns first using the DataExplorer package (profile_missing)
#use kbl function to make publishable table
df %>% 
  select(c(CRP_3SDWlg, IL_6plus1_lg, HDL_3SDWlg,
           BE_A1c_3SDWlg, t3bpsy_3SDWlg, t3bpdi_3SDWlg, 
           t3bmip_reco, t3cort_1_wzlg, t3cesd, t3anx)) %>% 
  profile_missing(.) %>% 
  mutate(pct_missing = round(pct_missing, 3)) %>% 
  arrange(desc(pct_missing)) %>% 
  kbl(caption = "Prevalence of Missing Data") %>% 
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE)
Prevalence of Missing Data
feature num_missing pct_missing
t3cort_1_wzlg 303 0.502
CRP_3SDWlg 279 0.462
IL_6plus1_lg 279 0.462
HDL_3SDWlg 279 0.462
BE_A1c_3SDWlg 279 0.462
t3bmip_reco 279 0.462
t3bpsy_3SDWlg 270 0.447
t3bpdi_3SDWlg 270 0.447
t3cesd 270 0.447
t3anx 270 0.447 
##the missing data percentage is relative high as this dataset combined Wave1-Wave 3 data. Half participants dropped at Wave 3 compared with Wave 1

#check how many cases have complete data in all variables 
samplesize <- df %>% 
  select(c(CRP_3SDWlg, IL_6plus1_lg, HDL_3SDWlg,
           BE_A1c_3SDWlg, t3bpsy_3SDWlg, t3bpdi_3SDWlg, 
           t3bmip_reco, t3cort_1_wzlg, t3cesd, t3anx)) %>% 
  filter(complete.cases(.)) %>% 
  nrow()
##282 complete cases

#creat sub-dataset for complete samples
df1 <- df %>%
  filter(complete.cases(select(., CRP_3SDWlg, IL_6plus1_lg, HDL_3SDWlg,
                               BE_A1c_3SDWlg, t3bpsy_3SDWlg, t3bpdi_3SDWlg, 
                               t3bmip_reco, t3cort_1_wzlg, t3cesd, t3anx)))
View(df1)
nrow(df1)
## [1] 282

3.2 Demographic characteristics transfer & summary

df1 <- df1 %>% 
  mutate(t1gender = factor(t1gender,
                           levels = c(0, 1),
                           labels = c("female", "male")))
Freq(df1$t1gender)

  sum(df1$t1gender == "female", na.rm = TRUE)/samplesize, accuracy = 0.1)

df1 <- df1 %>% 
  mutate(t1born = factor(t1born,

3.3 Summary for variables using for LPA

I want to make a APA style table summarizing all variables used for LPA. To achieve that, I conducted several steps including: 1) select variables 2) pivot to long format with variable name as one column and value as another column 3) summary the data 4) create apa format table

#take a gimps of all variables  
df1 %>% 
  select(c(CRP_3SDWlg, IL_6plus1_lg, HDL_3SDWlg,
           BE_A1c_3SDWlg, t3bpsy_3SDWlg, t3bpdi_3SDWlg, 
           t3bmip_reco, t3cesd, t3anx)) %>%
  summary(across(everything(.))) 
##    CRP_3SDWlg     IL_6plus1_lg       HDL_3SDWlg    BE_A1c_3SDWlg

##  1st Qu.:3.584   1st Qu.:0.06508   1st Qu.:1.699   1st Qu.:0.7076  
##  Median :3.969   Median :0.12821   Median :1.813   Median :0.7243  
##  Mean   :4.029   Mean   :0.11611   Mean   :1.790   Mean   :0.7252  
##  3rd Qu.:4.458   3rd Qu.:0.16579   3rd Qu.:1.903   3rd Qu.:0.7404  
##  Max.   :5.461   Max.   :0.53479   Max.   :2.110   Max.   :0.8494  
##  t3bpsy_3SDWlg   t3bpdi_3SDWlg    t3bmip_reco         t3cesd     
##  Min.   :1.916   Min.   :1.620   Min.   :0.0000   Min.   :1.000  
##  1st Qu.:2.025   1st Qu.:1.778   1st Qu.:0.5602   1st Qu.:1.300  
##  Median :2.059   Median :1.820   Median :0.8315   Median :1.450  
##  Mean   :2.063   Mean   :1.818   Mean   :0.7261   Mean   :1.535  
##  3rd Qu.:2.098   3rd Qu.:1.859   3rd Qu.:0.9568   3rd Qu.:1.700  
##  Max.   :2.198   Max.   :1.974   Max.   :1.0000   Max.   :3.650  
##      t3anx     
##  Min.   :1.00  
##  1st Qu.:1.25  
##  Median :1.75  
##  Mean   :1.83  
##  3rd Qu.:2.25  
##  Max.   :4.00
df_plot <- df1 %>% 
  select(c(CRP_3SDWlg, IL8w_1lg, IL_6plus1_lg, HDL_3SDWlg,
           BE_A1c_3SDWlg, t3bpsy_3SDWlg, t3bpdi_3SDWlg, 
           t3bmip_reco, t3cesd, t3anx)) %>% 
  rename(., 
         "CRP" = "CRP_3SDWlg",
         "IL8" = "IL8w_1lg",
         "IL6" = "IL_6plus1_lg",
         "HDL" = "HDL_3SDWlg",
         "A1c" = "BE_A1c_3SDWlg",
         "systolic_bp" = "t3bpsy_3SDWlg",
         "diastolic_dp" = "t3bpdi_3SDWlg",
         "BMI_percentile" = "t3bmip_reco",
         "depressive_symptoms" = "t3cesd",
         "anxiety" = "t3anx") %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(),
               names_to = "variables",
               values_to = "values")
View(df_plot)

# make publishable table
descriptive <- df_plot %>% 
  group_by(variables) %>% 
  summarise(
    Mean = mean(values, na.rm = TRUE),
    Median = median(values, na.rm = TRUE),
    Min = min(values, na.rm = TRUE),
    Max = max(values, na.rm = TRUE),
    SD = sd(values, na.rm = TRUE))

descriptive %>% 
  kable(caption = 
          "Table 1. Descriptive statistics of variables",
        digits = 3,
        align = "l") %>% ##
  kable_classic_2(full_width = FALSE)

3.4 Visualization of variables

Here I want to plot all of my variables using for LPA to check of there are any mistakes.

3.5 Run latent profile analysis in R using tidyLPA package

Here is how it looks like when using MPLUS to do LPA, which is really time-consuming (See piscture below). tidyLPA package developed by Rosenberg et al.(2018) can achieved the same LPA analyses with really simple codes.

LPA is based on how we choose the variance & covariance matrix. In this study, the variances were set to varying across different profiles and covariance were set to zero to assist interpretation. The matrix looks like: (Pastor et al., 2007)

model <- df1%>% 
  select(c(CRP_3SDWlg, IL_6plus1_lg, HDL_3SDWlg,
           BE_A1c_3SDWlg, t3bpsy_3SDWlg, t3bpdi_3SDWlg, 
           t3bmip_reco, t3cesd, t3anx)) %>% 
  estimate_profiles(1:6, package = "mplus", ANALYSIS = "starts = 200, 50;", 
                    variances = "varying", covariances = "zero")
## Warning: 
## One or more analyses resulted in warnings! Examine these analyses carefully: model_2_class_6
model
## tidyLPA analysis using mplus: 
## 
##  Model Classes AIC      BIC      Entropy prob_min prob_max n_min n_max BLRT_p
##  2     1       -2249.10 -2183.54 1.00    1.00     1.00     1.00  1.00        
##  2     2       -2658.49 -2523.74 0.87    0.96     0.97     0.36  0.64  0.00  
##  2     3       -2831.84 -2627.90 0.83    0.88     0.97     0.22  0.44  0.00  
##  2     4       -2906.12 -2632.98 0.85    0.86     0.97     0.14  0.36  0.00  
##  2     5       -2943.28 -2600.94 0.85    0.90     0.96     0.16  0.29  0.00  
##  2     6       -2984.22 -2572.68 0.85    0.84     0.95     0.10  0.27  0.00
plot(model)

?estimate_profiles

model[["model_2_class_4"]][["dff"]][['Class']] %>% 
  table() %>% 
  prop.table()
## .
##         1         2         3         4 
## 0.1666667 0.3581560 0.1382979 0.3368794
#seems the 4-profile solution is the best, so i choose it as my final solution
model4 <- model[4]

3.6 Compare different biomaker values across profiles

After LPA, we hope to see variable values in different profiles and if there are significant difference on each variables. Therefore, anova was conducted to compare values of each variables.

In this section, I used a handy table1 function to directly output the table describing the variables and the difference between them.

profiledata <- get_data(model4[[1]])
View(profiledata)
profiledata$Class <- factor(profiledata$Class, 
                              levels = c(1, 2, 3, 4))
Freq(profiledata$Class)
## Frequency Statistics:
## ───────────
##      N    %
## ───────────
## 1   47 16.7
## 2  101 35.8
## 3   39 13.8
## 4   95 33.7
## ───────────
## Total N = 282
pvalue_anova <- function(x, ...) {
  # Construct vectors of data y, and groups (strata) g
  y <- unlist(x)
  g <- factor(rep(1:length(x), times=sapply(x, length)))
  p <- anova(lm(y ~ g ))$`Pr(>F)`[1]
  c("", sub("<", "&lt;", format.pval(p, digits=3, eps=0.001)))
  }

my.render.cont <- function(x) {
  # Apply rounding and formatting to continuous variables, rounding all to 3 digits
  rounded_stats <- stats.apply.rounding(stats.default(x), digits = 3)
  
  # Ensure that the stats are numeric before formatting
  MEAN <- as.numeric(rounded_stats$MEAN)
  SD <- as.numeric(rounded_stats$SD)
  MEDIAN <- as.numeric(rounded_stats$MEDIAN)
  
  c(" " = "",  # Leave a blank line for the variable name
    "Mean (SD)" = sprintf("%3.3f (&plusmn; %3.3f)", MEAN, SD),
    "Median" = sprintf("%3.3f", MEDIAN))
}


table1::table1(~ CRP_3SDWlg + IL_6plus1_lg + HDL_3SDWlg + 
                 BE_A1c_3SDWlg + t3bpsy_3SDWlg + t3bpdi_3SDWlg +
                 t3bmip_reco + t3cesd + t3anx | Class, 
               data = profiledata,
               overall = FALSE,  # Don't include overall stats
               render.continuous = my.render.cont,
               extra.col = list(`P-value for ANOVA`=pvalue_anova))
1
(N=47)		 2
(N=101)		 3
(N=39)		 4
(N=95)		 P-value for ANOVA

3.7 Visualization

In this section, I want to visualize the result and give a more direct picture showing the difference between profiles. Therefore, ggplot together with bar plot was used to complete this.

long_profiledata <- profiledata %>% 
  select(c(CRP_3SDWlg, IL_6plus1_lg, HDL_3SDWlg,
         BE_A1c_3SDWlg, t3bpsy_3SDWlg, t3bpdi_3SDWlg, 
         t3bmip_reco, t3cesd, t3anx, Class)) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = -Class, names_to = "variable", values_to = "value") 

View(long_profiledata)

long_profiledata$Class <- factor(long_profiledata$Class,
                                 levels = c(1,2,3,4),
                                 labels = c("group1",
                                            "group2",
                                            "group3",
                                            "group4"))

long_profiledata$variable <- factor(long_profiledata$variable,
                                    levels = c("CRP_3SDWlg", "IL_6plus1_lg", "HDL_3SDWlg", 
                                               "BE_A1c_3SDWlg", "t3bpsy_3SDWlg",
                                               "t3bpdi_3SDWlg", 
                                               "t3bmip_reco", "t3cesd", "t3anx"))

long_profiledata %>% 
  group_by(Class, variable) %>%
  summarise(Mean = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = 'drop') %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = variable, y = Mean, fill = Class)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("pink", "lightblue", "lightgreen", "lightyellow")) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),  # Rotate the x-axis labels for better readability
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),               # Adjust font size of axis text
    axis.title = element_text(size = 12)               # Adjust font size of axis titles
  )

4 Bibliography

Lamers, F., Milaneschi, Y., Vinkers, C. H., Schoevers, R. A., Giltay, E. J., & Penninx, B. W. (2020). Depression profilers and immuno-metabolic dysregulation: Longitudinal results from the NESDA study. Brain, Behavior, and Immunity, 88, 174-183.

Rosenberg, J. M., Beymer, P. N., Anderson, D. J., Van Lissa, C. J., & Schmidt, J. A. (2019). tidyLPA: An R package to easily carry out latent profile analysis (LPA) using open-source or commercial software. Journal of Open Source Software, 3(30), 978.

Pastor, D. A., Barron, K. E., Miller, B. J., & Davis, S. L. (2007). A latent profile analysis of college students’ achievement goal orientation. Contemporary Educational Psychology, 32(1), 8–47.
